response:
Call update_projects with next_review_date set directly.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-abc", "next_review_date": "2026-04-15"}])

The next_review_date parameter lets you override the computed next review date regardless of last_reviewed or review_interval.

Call update_projects to enable completed_by_children.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-xyz", "completed_by_children": true}])

This tells OmniFocus to automatically mark the project complete when all its tasks are done.

No tool call needed — the data is already available. Call get_tasks with the task's ID and check the nextDueDate field in the response. For recurring tasks, get_tasks returns nextDueDate, nextDeferDate, and nextPlannedDate showing the next occurrence's dates without needing to complete the current one.

get_tasks(task_id="<the task's ID>")

Look at the nextDueDate field in the result.

Use get_projects with stalled_only=True.

get_projects(stalled_only=true)

This returns active projects with no available next actions — exactly the ones that are stuck or need new tasks added.

This is an interpretation question. First, get the task to check its catchUpAutomatically field:

get_tasks(query="Take vitamins")

Check the catchUpAutomatically field in the response:
- If catchUpAutomatically is true: completing it creates ONE catch-up occurrence (not 5 separate overdue tasks).
- If catchUpAutomatically is false: each missed interval spawns its own occurrence, so you could get multiple overdue tasks.

No further tool call needed — just read the field and explain the behavior.

This is an interpretation/warning question. First check whether the Priority parent tag has childrenAreMutuallyExclusive set:

get_tags()

Look at the Priority parent tag's childrenAreMutuallyExclusive field. If true, assigning 'Low' will silently remove 'High' — OmniFocus enforces mutual exclusivity automatically. If false, both tags would coexist.

Warn the user about this behavior before they proceed.

Call create_projects with due_date and defer_date.

create_projects([{"name": "Q3 Report", "folder_path": "Work", "due_date": "2026-07-15", "defer_date": "2026-06-01"}])

defer_date makes the project hidden/unavailable until June 1. due_date sets the July 15 deadline.

Call update_projects with due_date set to empty string to clear it.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-abc", "due_date": ""}])

Call get_projects with the project_id to retrieve its dates.

get_projects(project_id="proj-xyz")

The response includes dueDate, deferDate, and plannedDate fields.

Call update_tasks to reparent the task under the new parent task.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-101", "parent_task_id": "task-200"}])

This moves task-101 from the inbox to become a subtask of task-200. Note: project_id and parent_task_id are mutually exclusive on create, but update_tasks accepts parent_task_id to reparent.

Call get_tasks with a query search.

get_tasks(query="mortgage")

This searches task names and notes case-insensitively across all projects.

Call get_tags to retrieve all tags with their parent info.

get_tags()

Look for the tag named 'High' in the results. The parentTagId field will show its parent tag's ID. Cross-reference that ID with the 'Energy' tag to confirm the nesting.

Call update_tasks with status='dropped'.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-500", "status": "dropped"}])

This marks the task as dropped (abandoned) without deleting it. It remains in the database but is hidden from active views.

Call update_projects with status='dropped' for all three.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-A", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-B", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-C", "status": "dropped"}])

No tool call needed — this is an interpretation question. The server instructions explain: completed/dropped reflect the task's OWN state, not its container's. A task in a completed project still shows completed: false if the task itself was never individually completed. However, available will be false because the container is done. Use available_only=True to filter these out.

If the user is seeing these tasks in a default get_tasks call, they should add available_only=true to exclude tasks in completed projects.

Call reorder_project to move proj-C before proj-A.

reorder_project(project_id="proj-C", before_project_id="proj-A")

Three calls needed. First create the parent tag with mutual exclusivity, then create the child tags.

1. create_tags([{"name": "Priority", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}])

2. create_tags([{"name": "High", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Medium", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Low", "parent_tag": "Priority"}])

The children_are_mutually_exclusive flag ensures that assigning one child tag automatically removes any sibling tags from the task.

Call update_tags to enable mutual exclusivity on the existing parent tag.

update_tags([{"id": "tag-energy-001", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}])

This makes it so assigning one child energy tag will automatically remove any sibling energy tags from a task.

Call update_projects to flag and tag the project in one call.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-website-001", "flagged": true, "add_tags": ["High Priority"]}])

Call reorder_task to move the QA task before the frontend task.

reorder_task(task_id="task-qa-001", before_task_id="task-fe-001")

Call delete_tags to permanently remove the tag.

delete_tags(tag_ids="tag-errands-001")

This deletes the tag entirely. Tasks that had it will lose the tag association but won't be deleted themselves.

Call get_perspectives to list all perspectives.

get_perspectives()

Filter the results for type='custom' to show only custom perspectives (excluding built-in ones).

Call create_folders with parent_path.

create_folders([{"name": "Q2", "parent_path": "Work"}])

Call switch_perspective.

switch_perspective(perspective_name="Weekly Review")

Two steps. First retrieve overdue tasks, then flag the most urgent.

1. get_tasks(overdue=true)

2. After reviewing results and identifying the 3 most urgent (by due date or context), flag them:

update_tasks([{"id": "<task-id-1>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<task-id-2>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<task-id-3>", "flagged": true}])

The task IDs would come from the get_tasks response, sorted by due date to identify the most urgent.

First search for the project, then get its tasks if it exists.

1. get_projects(query="Quantum Computing Research")

2. If a project is found, use its ID:
   get_tasks(project_id="<project-id-from-step-1>")

If no project is found in step 1, inform the user that no such project exists.

Call create_tasks with tags as a list. No project_id means inbox.

create_tasks([{"task_name": "Buy birthday gift", "tags": ["Computer", "Errands"]}])

Call create_tasks with all 5 tasks in a single batch. No project_id means inbox.

create_tasks([{"task_name": "Buy groceries"}, {"task_name": "Do laundry"}, {"task_name": "Wash dishes"}, {"task_name": "Vacuum living room"}, {"task_name": "Prep meals for the week"}])

Call create_projects with both projects in a single batch.

create_projects([{"name": "Kitchen Renovation", "project_type": "sequential", "folder_path": "Home"}, {"name": "Garden Planning", "project_type": "parallel", "folder_path": "Home"}])

Create a parent task as a sequential action group, then add subtasks under it.

1. create_tasks([{"task_name": "Event Planning", "project_id": "proj-100", "sequential": true}])

2. Using the returned task ID as parent:
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Research venues", "parent_task_id": "<parent-id>"}, {"task_name": "Book venue", "parent_task_id": "<parent-id>"}, {"task_name": "Send invitations", "parent_task_id": "<parent-id>"}])

The sequential=true on the parent makes it a sequential action group, so tasks must be completed in order.

Create a parallel project, then use sequential action groups for each dependency chain.

1. create_projects([{"name": "Home Renovation", "project_type": "parallel"}])

2. Using the project ID, create two sequential action groups:
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Contractor Work", "project_id": "<proj-id>", "sequential": true}, {"task_name": "Paint Selection", "project_id": "<proj-id>", "sequential": true}])

3. Add subtasks to each action group:
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Get contractor quotes", "parent_task_id": "<contractor-group-id>"}, {"task_name": "Schedule contractor", "parent_task_id": "<contractor-group-id>"}, {"task_name": "Choose paint colors", "parent_task_id": "<paint-group-id>"}, {"task_name": "Buy paint", "parent_task_id": "<paint-group-id>"}])

The parallel project allows both groups to proceed simultaneously, while each sequential group enforces the internal order.

Three steps, each depending on IDs from the previous.

1. Create the parent task as sequential:
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Prepare presentation", "project_id": "proj-200", "sequential": true}])

2. Create sequential subtasks under it (using returned ID):
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Research topic", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>"}, {"task_name": "Write outline", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>"}, {"task_name": "Create slides", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>", "sequential": false}])

Note: 'Create slides' has sequential=false to make its children parallel.

3. Add parallel subtasks under 'Create slides' (using its returned ID):
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Design template", "parent_task_id": "<slides-id>"}, {"task_name": "Add content", "parent_task_id": "<slides-id>"}, {"task_name": "Add animations", "parent_task_id": "<slides-id>"}])

OmniFocus doesn't support true diamond dependencies (one task waiting on multiple parallel predecessors). The closest approximation uses a sequential action group.

Approach: Create a sequential action group with a parallel sub-group for the independent tasks, followed by the dependent task.

1. Create a sequential parent task in the project:
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Report Analysis", "project_id": "proj-300", "sequential": true}])

2. Create a parallel sub-group for the independent tasks, plus the dependent task:
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Independent Work", "parent_task_id": "<parent-id>", "sequential": false}, {"task_name": "Review findings", "parent_task_id": "<parent-id>"}])

3. Add the independent tasks under the parallel sub-group:
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Draft report", "parent_task_id": "<independent-group-id>"}, {"task_name": "Collect data", "parent_task_id": "<independent-group-id>"}])

If completed_by_children is set on the parallel group, the sequential parent advances to 'Review findings' only after both independent tasks are done. Set it:
update_tasks([{"id": "<independent-group-id>", "completed_by_children": true}])

Per the server instructions: dropping a recurring task without clearing recurrence first spawns the next occurrence. To stop the series entirely, clear recurrence and drop in one call.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-weekly-123", "recurrence": "", "status": "dropped"}])

Call update_tags to move the tag under the People parent. Task associations are preserved automatically.

update_tags([{"id": "tag-aimee-001", "parent_tag": "People"}])
